title: वायु द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री वायु द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम् ॥

प्रथमं वायुमित्युक्तं द्वितीयं प्राणदायकम्।
तृतीयं मातरिश्वानं चतुर्थं पवनं विदुः॥१॥

पञ्चमं मरुतं नाम षष्ठं अनिलमेव च।
सप्तमं गन्धवाहं च अष्टमं शीघ्रगामिनम्॥२॥

नवमं सर्वगं चैव दशमं प्राणवायुकम्।
एकादशं शब्दवाहिनं द्वादशं तनूनपातम्॥३॥

द्वादशैतानि नामानि वायुदेवस्य कीर्तयेत्।
यः पठेत् प्रातरुत्थाय प्राणशक्तिं लभेत् सदा॥४॥

श्वासरोगो विनश्येत् च बलं वीर्यं च वर्धते।
वायुदेवप्रसादेन दीर्घायुश्च लभेन्नरः॥५॥

॥ इति श्री वायु द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Vayu Dwadash Nama Stotram ||

Prathamam Vayum-ityuktam Dvitiyam Prana-dayakam.
Tritiyam Matarishvanam Chaturtham Pavanam Viduh.||1||

Panchamam Marutam Nama Shashtham Anilam Eva Cha.
Saptamam Gandha-vaham Cha Ashtamam Shighra-gaminam.||2||

Navamam Sarva-gam Chaiva Dashamam Prana-vayukam.
Ekadasham Shabda-vahinam Dvadasham Tanu-napatam.||3||

Dvadashai-tani Namani Vayu-devasya Kirtayet.
Yah Pathet Pratar-utthaya Prana-shaktim Labhet Sada.||4||

Shvasa-rogo Vinashyet Cha Balam Viryam Cha Vardhate.
Vayu-deva-prasadena Dirghayush-cha Labhen-narah.||5||

|| Iti Shri Vayu Dwadash Nama Stotram Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning: प्रथम नाम वायु, दूसरा प्राणदायक, तीसरा मातरिश्वा, चौथा पवन। पाँचवाँ मरुत, छठा अनिल, सातवाँ गन्धवाह, आठवाँ शीघ्रगामी। नवाँ सर्वग, दसवाँ प्राणवायु, ग्यारहवाँ शब्दवाही, बारहवाँ तनूनपात। वायुदेव के इन बारह नामों का प्रातःकाल पाठ करने से प्राणशक्ति बढ़ती है, श्वासरोग नष्ट होता है, बल-वीर्य वर्धित होता है और दीर्घायु प्राप्त होती है।
benefits: Twelve Vedic names of Lord Vayu for pranashakti enhancement
Associated in tradition with respiratory diseases and breathing disorders
Invokes long life by the grace of vayu deva
Sacred for practitioners of Pranayama and Yoga
description: Among the most cherished prayers to Lord Vayu, Vayu Dwadash Nama Stotram (वायु द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम्) stands as a devotional hymn of praise. Rooted in Rig Veda, this composition carries deep spiritual significance. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to increase physical strength and vitality as well as cure respiratory diseases and breathing disorders. Additionally, it is known to grant long life by the grace of Vayu Deva. It is especially recommended on Saturday (Shanivar) and Tuesday (Mangalvar) and during Hanuman Jayanti for maximum spiritual benefit. Comprising 5 verses, it can be completed in approximately 3 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Vayu Dwadash Nama Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Vayu Dwadash Nama Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: The Vayu Dwadash Nama Stotram (वायु द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम्) is a 12-name devotional hymn dedicated to Vayu Bhagavan — the Vedic god of wind, breath (prana), and life force — who is one of the eight Vasus and one of the most important Vedic deities, honored from the Rigveda onwards. The twelve names typically chanted include Vayu, Anila, Pavana, Maruta, Marut, Sadagati, Pranesha, Prabhanjana, Gandhavaha, Akashachara, Vatasakha and Sparshana — each name highlighting a different attribute of the wind god (purifier, swift-mover, fragrance-carrier, sky-traveler, friend of fire, the touch-sense lord). The hymn is preserved in classical tradition and recited especially in the Madhva Vaishnava sampradaya which holds Vayu in unique theological reverence. (Source: classical Vedic Vayu upasana tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the benefits of chanting the Vayu Dwadash Nama Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: Devotees recite the Vayu Dwadash Nama for (1) physical strength, vitality and ojas — Vayu rules prana, and increasing prana through the right breath increases life-force; (2) cure or relief from respiratory ailments — asthma, bronchitis, persistent cough, COPD, and shortness of breath, since Vayu rules the lungs and breath; (3) management of vata-dosha imbalance in Ayurveda — joint pains, stiffness, neurological tremors, anxiety; (4) longevity, since prana-bala directly determines ayurdaya (lifespan); (5) success in pranayama and yoga sadhana; and (6) protection during travel, since Vayu is the deva of all motion. The stotram is often chanted with controlled breathing (anuloma-viloma or nadi shodhana pranayama) for compounded effect. (Sources: Vayu upasana paddhati; Ayurvedic vata management tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: When and how should the Vayu Dwadash Nama be chanted for best results?
seoFaqs.answer: Vayu Dwadash Nama is most powerfully chanted at dawn during pranayama practice — when the prana-vayu in the atmosphere is purest. Special days include Hanuman Jayanti (Chaitra Purnima or Kartik Krishna Chaturdashi), every Tuesday and Saturday (Hanuman's days, since Hanuman is Vayuputra), Madhva Navami / Madhva Jayanti for Madhva-tradition devotees (Madhvacharya is held to be the third Vayu-avatara after Hanuman and Bhima), and during periods of respiratory illness or vata-dosha flare-up. Recommended practice: 108 chants daily seated facing north or east, combined with Nadi Shodhana pranayama, with offerings of camphor (kapur), white flowers and incense to a Vayu symbol or to a Hanuman murti. (Sources: Vayu and Hanuman upasana paddhati; Madhva tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is Vayu so important in the Madhva Vaishnava tradition?
seoFaqs.answer: In the Madhva Dvaita Vedanta school founded by Sri Madhvacharya (1238–1317 CE), Vayu Bhagavan holds a uniquely exalted position as the principal devata — second only to Vishnu (Mukhya Pranadeva). Madhvacharya himself is regarded by his followers as the third incarnation of Vayu, after Hanuman (Treta Yuga) and Bhima (Dvapara Yuga), specifically sent by Vishnu to establish the Dvaita interpretation of Vedanta in the Kali Yuga. Hence every Madhva daily chants Vayu-stutis, recites the Hanuman Chalisa and Bhima-related verses, and concludes Sandhyavandana with prayers to Vayudeva. The Madhva mutts of Udupi (the Ashta Mathas) install Vayu symbolism alongside Krishna, and the tradition holds that bhakti to Vayu is the indispensable gateway to bhakti to Vishnu. (Sources: Madhva Vijaya by Narayana Pandita; Madhva Sampradaya tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the connection between Vayu, prana, and pranayama in yoga?
seoFaqs.answer: In classical yoga and Upanishadic philosophy, Vayu is not merely the external wind but the cosmic prana — the life-force that animates every living being. The Prashna Upanishad (3.1) states that prana arises from the Atman, just as a shadow arises from a person. Yoga divides this internal Vayu into five pranas (Prana, Apana, Samana, Udana, Vyana) and five sub-pranas (Naga, Kurma, Krikara, Devadatta, Dhananjaya) — together the dasha-vayus that govern every bodily function from breath and digestion to speech and sneezing. Pranayama is the science of regulating these vayus, and the Vayu Dwadash Nama is often chanted before pranayama to invoke the deity who governs the breath. Hatha Yoga Pradipika and the Patanjali Yoga Sutras both emphasize that mastery of vayu-prana leads directly to mastery of mind. (Sources: Prashna Upanishad 3; Hatha Yoga Pradipika 2; Patanjali Yoga Sutras 2.49–53.)

seoFaqs.question: What other Vayu and Hanuman stotras complement this hymn?
seoFaqs.answer: The Vayu Dwadash Nama is most often paired with (1) Hanuman Chalisa by Tulsidas — the universal 40-verse hymn to Hanuman the Vayuputra; (2) Hanuman Ashtaka / Sankat Mochan Hanuman Ashtak; (3) Bajrang Baan; (4) Hanuman Bahuk; (5) Vayu Stuti by Sri Trivikrama Pandita — the foundational Madhva-tradition stotra to Vayudeva (Vayu-Hanuman-Bhima-Madhva); (6) Bhima Stotra; and (7) the Rigveda Vayu Suktam (Rigveda 1.2 — Vayav A Yahi Darshateme Soma Aranktah) — the most ancient Vedic invocation of the wind god. A full Vayu sadhana typically combines morning pranayama, the Vedic Vayu Sukta, the Vayu Dwadasha Nama, and a Hanuman Chalisa recitation in the evening. (Sources: Tulsidas Hanuman Chalisa; Trivikrama Pandita Vayu Stuti; Rigveda 1.2.)
